questions.question_text: Which of the following may NOT be used as a Colorado trust or escrow account?
questions.correct_answer: A certificate of deposit with a fixed maturity term
questions.explanation: C.R.S. 12-10-217(1)(i) permits any checking, demand, passbook, or statement account insured by a U.S. government agency. Rule 5.6 prohibits placing money belonging to others in any account with a fixed maturity term or a fee/penalty for early withdrawal — funds must be available on demand — so CDs are not permitted.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a personal check into the brokerage trust account to cover a temporary shortfall. This is:
questions.explanation: Commingling personal funds with trust funds is prohibited (C.R.S. 12-10-217(1)(i); Rule 5.10). The one exception: a brokerage may deposit personal funds needed to cover bank charges on the account. Covering a shortfall with personal funds is not that exception — and a shortfall itself suggests trust funds were already converted.

questions.question_text: Under Commission Rule 5.2, a brokerage's separate account deposit agreement must identify the account's fiduciary nature using:
questions.correct_answer: The word 'trust' or 'escrow' plus a label identifying its purpose
distractors: The broker's license number and the word 'fiduciary'
The Real Estate Commission's name as co-owner
questions.explanation: Rule 5.2 requires the word 'trust' or 'escrow' together with a purpose label such as 'sales escrow' or 'security deposit escrow' in the deposit agreement with the recognized depository; Rule 5.3 requires the account be in the name of the brokerage firm (or the employing/independent broker if a sole proprietorship).

questions.question_text: Which brokers in Colorado must maintain a trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Every employing broker handling others' money
distractors: All licensed brokers regardless of duties
questions.explanation: Every employing broker who handles money belonging to others must maintain a trust account. If a broker never handles client funds, no trust account is required.

questions.question_text: A Colorado trust account must be maintained at:
distractors: A bank within the broker's county
questions.explanation: Trust accounts must be held at a Colorado financial institution. An out-of-state bank, even if FDIC-insured, would not satisfy the requirement.

questions.question_text: A brokerage firm holding earnest money must deposit it into its trust or escrow account no later than:
questions.correct_answer: Three business days following receipt or mutual execution of the contract, whichever is later
distractors: Ten calendar days after mutual execution
questions.explanation: Rule 5.7.B requires money belonging to others (other than property-management receipts, which get five business days under Rule 5.7.A) to be deposited no later than three business days following receipt of funds or mutual execution of the contract, whichever is later. Under the Commission-approved contract, earnest money is tendered with the contract unless an Alternative Earnest Money Deadline is agreed.

questions.question_text: A broker delivers earnest money to the title company named as earnest money holder. Under Rule 5.12, the broker must obtain:
questions.explanation: When a broker delivers earnest money to the earnest money holder named in the contract — often a title company — Rule 5.12.A requires the broker to obtain a dated, signed receipt. The contract designates the earnest money holder, which may instead be the brokerage's own trust account.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a contract on Monday specifying delivery of earnest money within 5 business days. The earnest money is due by:
questions.explanation: Five business days from Monday would be the following Monday (Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Mon). The contract timeline controls when specified.

questions.question_text: A broker receives earnest money but delays depositing it for two weeks without cause. This delay:
questions.correct_answer: May violate trust account delivery rules
distractors: Is permitted if the check does not bounce
Only matters if a complaint is filed
questions.explanation: Rule 5.7.B requires deposit no later than three business days following receipt of funds or mutual execution of the contract, whichever is later. An unexplained two-week delay violates the rule and is grounds for discipline.

questions.question_text: The contract specifies that earnest money will be held by the title company. The listing broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Ensure delivery to the title company
distractors: Deposit it in their own trust account
Return it to the buyer for safekeeping
questions.explanation: The broker must follow the contract terms. If the contract specifies the title company as the custodian, the earnest money should be delivered there, not the broker's trust account.

questions.question_text: How long must a Colorado broker retain trust account records after closing?
questions.explanation: Records must be retained four years, beginning from the consummation (closing) date of the transaction, or from the expiration date of any listing contract that does not consummate (C.R.S. 12-10-217(1)(k); Rule 6.20).

questions.question_text: Trust account reconciliations in Colorado must be performed:
questions.explanation: Brokers must perform monthly reconciliations of trust account records, including receipts, disbursements, and running balances.

questions.question_text: The Commission announces it will audit a broker's trust account. Can the broker refuse?
questions.correct_answer: No — Commission can audit at any time
distractors: Yes, unless a complaint was filed
Yes, if the broker hires an attorney
questions.explanation: The Commission has the authority to audit trust accounts at any time. Refusing an audit could result in additional disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A listed property never goes under contract and the listing expires. The broker must retain the transaction file for:
questions.correct_answer: 4 years from the listing's expiration date
questions.explanation: Rule 6.20 requires transaction records to be kept for four years, measured from the consummation (closing) date or, for listings that never consummate, from the listing's expiration date. Files for failed transactions must still be retained.

questions.question_text: Which records must a Colorado broker maintain for each transaction?
distractors: Only trust account deposits and withdrawals
questions.explanation: Complete transaction records include listing agreements, contracts, correspondence, disclosures, and closing documents — not just a single document.
